text: The surgeon examines the anatomical structures, noting the adhesion of the right fallopian tube to the right-sided ovarian cyst, while the left-sided tube and ovary appear normal. Using a monopolar hook, the surgeon coagulates and dissects the cortex of the tense ovarian cyst.

The surgeon uses two non-tooth graspers to dissect the cyst wall from the ovarian tissue, carefully separating them along a proper plane. The uneven surface thickness of the cyst wall leads to its rupture during the procedure, highlighting the importance of separating the cyst wall before rupture for clearer anatomical visualization.

The cyst wall is visibly thin, increasing the likelihood of rupture. The cyst ruptures, and the surgeon inserts a sucker tube into the cyst to suction the cystic fluid. The fluid is collected outside the abdomen using a sterile syringe for microscopic examination. A tooth holding grasper and a non-tooth grasper are then used to dissect the cyst wall from the ovarian tissue.

The surgeon gently peels the cystic wall from the ovarian tissue, aiming to minimize blood loss by maintaining a proper dissection plane. Blood vessels in the remaining cystic wall are coagulated before cutting and separating it from the ovarian tissue. The ovarian tissue is washed with normal saline to identify and coagulate mild bleeding points using bipolar cautery, taking care to avoid over coagulation to prevent tissue damage.

The surgeon places two or three stitches on the ovarian tissue for hemostasis and to prevent adhesion formation after the removal of a large ovarian tumor. Excess suture material is removed from the port site. The ovarian tissue is extracted from the abdomen. Suction and irrigation are performed repeatedly to remove all blood clots from the surgical field.
